school: The critical link between Ibrahim ibn Adham's ascetic tradition and the formal Chishti order; brought the Sufi path from Syria to Chisht (in modern Afghanistan) and trained Abu Ahmad Chishti, who gave the order its name; his transmission created the lineage through which Moinuddin Chishti would eventually take the path to India — making him the grandfather of the entire Indian Chishti tradition

books.title: Transmitted teachings (oral tradition)
books.note: No written works attributed; transmission was oral through the Chishti silsila

school: Critical link in the Naqshbandi silsila between Bayazid Bistami and Yusuf Hamadani; known for his doctrine of universal spiritual hospitality — the inscription above his hospice door read: 'Whoever enters this house, give them bread; ask not about their faith'; his sayings were compiled in Nūr al-ʿUlūm and deeply influenced Rumi and other Persian mystics

books.title: Nur al-Ulum (Sayings of Kharaqani)
books.note: Compiled teachings emphasising love, service, and universal hospitality

school: Khorasan / Naqshbandi Lineage — Bridge figure between the Baghdad school and the Naqshbandiyya and Yeseviyya

Pivotal figure linking the Khorasani Sufi tradition to both the Naqshbandiyya and Yeseviyya orders; both Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani (founder of the Khwajagan, direct precursor to Naqshbandiyya) and Ahmad Yesevi (founder of Yeseviyya) were his students; his school in Merv was a major centre of Islamic learning in Central Asia

books.title: Rutbat al-Hayat
books.note: Stages of spiritual life — key text of early Khwajagan tradition

school: The actual founder of the Suhrawardi Sufi school — his nephew Shihab al-Din al-Suhrawardi is more famous, but Abu Najib established the foundational framework; his Adab al-Muridin (Etiquette of the Seekers) was the first systematic manual for Sufi conduct and became the blueprint for subsequent Sufi orders; key link between the Baghdad Junayd school and the institutionalised Suhrawardiyya

books.title: Adab al-Muridin
books.url: https://archive.org/search?query=adab+muridin+suhrawardi
books.note: First systematic manual of Sufi conduct — blueprint for all subsequent Sufi orders

school: Naqshbandiyya — Formulated the 8 foundational principles (kalimat-i qudsiyya) of the Naqshbandi path

books.title: Risala-yi Sahibiyya (Letter to his Master)
books.note: Contains the Eight Naqshbandi Principles that define the order to this day.

school: Qadiriyya / Maghrebi Sufism — teacher of Ibn Arabi's masters; the most important Sufi of the Muslim West

books.title: Uns al-Wahid (Intimate Companion of the Solitary)
books.url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abu_Madyan
books.note: The most important Sufi master of the Muslim West; his lineage connects to Ibn Arabi

books.title: Wikipedia — Abu Madyan
books.url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abu_Madyan

quotes: Whoever truly seeks God finds that God was seeking him first.
The veil between you and God is not God — it is yourself.
Contentment is a treasure that never runs out.

school: Moroccan School / Shadhiliyya Founding — Murdered on his mountain; his student founded the Shadhiliyya

books.title: Al-Salat al-Mashishiyya (The Mashishi Prayer)
books.url: https://archive.org/search?query=Salat%20al-Mashishiyya%20Ibn%20Mashish%20prayer
books.note: One of the most revered Sufi prayers in the world — recited daily by millions in the Shadhili tradition.

school: The spiritual master of Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili and thus the grandfather-figure of the entire Shadhiliyya order; his Salat al-Mashishiyya (Prayer of Ibn Mashish) is one of the most widely recited devotional prayers in the Islamic world, used daily by Shadhili followers across five continents; a descendant of the Prophet through Idris I of Morocco

books.title: Al-Salat al-Mashishiyya

books.note: The most celebrated Shadhili devotional prayer, recited globally

school: Akbarian — student of Ibn Arabi's circle; known for shamahid (witnessing beauty) as spiritual practice

school: Suhrawardiyya Order — South Asian Founder

quotes: The heart that loves God has no room for pride.
Service to the poor is service to God — there is no shorter path.
The disciple who serves his master has already begun to serve God.

books.title: Diwan (Persian-Sindhi verse — partially extant)
books.note: His shrine in Sehwan Sharif is one of Pakistan's most visited; the dhammal dance performed there is iconic.

school: Chishti Order (Punjab) — Teacher of Nizamuddin Auliya; his Punjabi verses enshrined in the Sikh Guru Granth Sahib

books.note: His Punjabi verses are in the Sikh scripture (Guru Granth Sahib) — the only Muslim saint so honoured.

quotes: Pride is the veil between the servant and God — lower it and the light enters.
The dervish who needs nothing is richer than the king who has everything.
Whoever serves the creation has served the Creator.

quotes: Past and future veil God from our sight — burn both of them with fire.

school: Chishti — third generation Chishti master in India; patron of simple living; refused all royal gifts

school: Chishti — earliest Sufi poet to write in Hindi (Hindawi); bridge between Arabic Sufism and Indian vernacular literature

school: One of the earliest Sufi missionaries in Bengal; established the Suhrawardiyya presence in eastern India decades before the Chishti order arrived; his khanqah at Pandua (near modern Malda) became the first major centre of Islamic learning in Bengal; credited with mass conversions through spiritual charisma; his tomb remains a major pilgrimage site

books.title: Maktubat (Letters)
books.note: Spiritual letters to disciples — primary record of Suhrawardi teaching in Bengal

school: Cousin and principal khalifa of Baba Farid; founded the Sabiri branch of the Chishti order, known for its intense austerity and Jalali temperament contrasting with the Nizami branch's accessibility; his shrine at Kalyar Sharif near Roorkee remains one of the most visited Sufi shrines in India; his spiritual lineage continued through figures like Ashraf Jahangir Semnani

books.title: Maktubat and Malfuzat (collected sayings)

books.title: Oral teachings (transmitted through Ibn Ata Allah's Lata'if al-Minan)
books.note: His mosque in Alexandria is still a major shrine; his teachings were recorded by Ibn Ata Allah.

school: Chishti Order (Delhi) — Teacher of Amir Khusrow

quotes: If there is a Paradise on this earth, it is here, it is here, it is here.
Love for God is the foundation — and everything else is built upon it.
The heart is the throne of God — do not let anyone other than God sit upon it.
Humility is the crown of the saints — whoever wears it has already arrived.
Serve all people regardless of their faith — God’s mercy is wider than any religion.

school: The most politically influential Naqshbandi master; effectively the power behind the Timurid throne in Central Asia; his vast landholdings and network of followers made the Naqshbandiyya the dominant spiritual force from Transoxiana to Herat; his Faqarat is a key text of Naqshbandi spiritual practice; direct link between Baha-ud-Din Naqshband and Ahmad Sirhindi in the silsila

Khalwatiyya-Naqshbandiyya — greatest Sufi of the Makassar kingdom; exiled by the Dutch to Sri Lanka, then Cape Town where he spread Islam in South Africa

school: Naqshbandiyya-Mujaddidiyya (Delhi) — Martyred; assassinated 1781 for his view that Hindu scriptures contained divine wisdom

The primary successor of Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan and one of the most influential Naqshbandi masters of 19th-century India; his Maqamat-i-Mazhari documents his teacher's spiritual stations; under his guidance the Delhi khanqah became the main centre transmitting the Mujaddidi branch of Naqshbandiyya; trained hundreds of khalifas who spread the order across India, the Arab world, and Southeast Asia

books.title: Maqamat-i-Mazhari
books.note: Biography and spiritual stations of Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan — key Mujaddidi text

school: Influential reformist Sufi master whose students founded several major African orders; emphasised direct attachment to the Prophet (al-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyya) over affiliation to any particular silsila; his students founded the Sanusiyya (Muhammad al-Sanusi), the Khatmiyya (Muhammad Uthman al-Mirghani), and the Rashidiyya orders, reshaping Islam across Sudan, Libya, Somalia, and West Africa

books.title: Iqaz al-Himam
books.note: Awakening of Spiritual Aspirations — core text of the Muhammadan path

quotes: The Prophet is the sun — we are the moons that reflect his light.
Purify the intention and you will find the action follows naturally.
Seek God in your every breath — for every breath that passes without remembrance is a loss.

school: Founded the Sanusiyya order in Cyrenaica (Libya) in 1837, establishing a network of zawiya lodges across the Sahara that served simultaneously as mosques, schools, law courts, and trading posts; his movement provided the organisational basis for Libyan resistance to Italian colonisation and later the Libyan state; combined Shadhili spirituality with Hanbali-influenced reform theology

books.title: Al-Salsabil al-Muin
books.note: Foundational Sanusiyya Sufi manual outlining the path and litanies

books.title: Al-Iqaz al-Wasnani
books.note: On the origins and principles of the Muhammadan path

quotes: The first obligation upon the Muslim is to know God — and to know God is to know His attributes.
Creed is the foundation — all acts of worship built on a cracked foundation will not stand.
The scholar who does not act on his knowledge is like a lamp that gives no light.

school: Abu Abdullah Muhammad al-Arabi al-Darqawi (Arabic: محمد العربي الدرقاوي; 1760–1823) was a Moroccan Sufi leader of the Shadhili tariqa and the author of letters concerning the dhikr he preached and instructions for daily life. He stressed noninvolvement in worldly affairs (Dunya) and spoke against other Sufi orders exploiting claims of barakah (blessings). He was imprisoned by the Moroccan ruler Mulay Slimane (r. 1792–1822) for supporting revolts against the throne, but was released by Abderrahmane (r. 1822–1859). A branch of the Shadhili order, the Darqawa, was organized around his teachings after his death, with members coming from a wide range of social groups. Though the Darqawa was once the most important tariqah in Morocco, its power waned as it spread throughout North Africa. His tomb is in the Zawiya Bou Brih also in the Rif.

Abū Bakr 'Abdollāh b. Moḥammad b. Šahāvar b. Anūšervān al-Rāzī (Persian: نجم‌الدین رازی) commonly known by the laqab, or sobriquet, of Najm al-Dīn Dāya, meaning "wetnurse" (573 AH/1177 – 654 AH/1256) was a 13th-century Sufi. Hamid Algar, translator of the Persian Merṣād to English, states the application of "wetnurse" to the author of the Merṣād derives from the idea of the initiate on the Path being a newborn infant who needs suckling to survive. Dāya followed the Sufi order, Kubrawiyya, established by one of his greatest influences, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Dāya traveled to Kārazm and soon became a morīd (pupil, one who follows the shaykh master and learns from him, undergoing spiritual training) of Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Kubrā then appointed Shaikh Majd al-Dīn Bagdādī as the spiritual trainer who also became Dāya's biggest influence. Dāya constantly refers to al-Dīn Bagdādī as "our shaikh." When his master, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā, was murdered in 618/1221, Dāya fled to Hamadan, then to Ardabil, and then to Anatolia where he finally settled with a fellow contemporary master Rumi.

Imam Hassan Ukhra Muhammad Azeem Barkhiya (1898 – 27 January 1979) (Urdu: امام حسن اخرٰی محمد عظیم برخیا), commonly referred to as Qalandar Baba Auliya (Urdu: قلندر بابا اولیاء), was the founding Imam and Spiritual Master of the Azeemiyya Sufi Order, which takes its name from him. He was a Scholar of Tassawuf, and is considered to be of the Abdāl, commonly being known by his honorific "Abdāl Haqq" (Urdu: ابدال حق). He was a student of Tajuddin Baba. He appointed Khawaja Shamsuddin Azeemi as head of the Silsila, and passed on 27 January 1979. Every January, his Urs (Death Anniversary) is conducted in which members of the Silsila congregate to recite the Quran and conduct gathering on matters of Tassawuf. His resting place is located in the Shadman district of Karachi, Pakistan.
